title: Project Artifacts & Governance
summary: The documents that authorise, control, and track a project - and the governance that keeps them aligned to value. Tailored to PMBOK 8, where Governance is now a full domain in its own right and artifacts provide the evidence behind the Governance and Scope domains.

sections.heading: Why artifacts and governance matter
sections.body: In PMBOK 8, artifacts are chosen to fit the project and are the evidence behind the domains they serve: the Scope domain (are we producing the right, accepted deliverables?) and the Monitoring & Controlling work that tells us whether we have reliable status to decide. Governance is now a full domain in its own right - nine processes covering integration, change control, knowledge, quality assurance, sourcing and close-out - and it is the authority framework that decides who approves what and how issues escalate.

blocks.title: How to read this guide
lines: Artifacts = the evidence: logs, registers, plans, baselines, reports.
Governance = the rules of the road: who decides, how issues escalate.
Monitoring & Controlling relies on status artifacts; the Scope domain relies on acceptance and change control.
Tailor the set to the project - there is no one mandatory list.

sections.heading: The project charter
sections.body: The charter formally authorises the project to exist and gives the project manager authority to apply organisational resources. Without it, the PM has no mandate. It is deliberately high-level and is created at the very start, before detailed planning.

rows.value: Project purpose and high-level objectives.
High-level scope and key deliverables.
Key milestones and an order-of-magnitude summary budget.
Key risks, assumptions and constraints.
Success criteria and the named sponsor.

blocks.title: Who signs it
lines: Signed or issued by the sponsor or initiator - someone external to the project with authority to fund it.
The PM is named in it, not the approver of it.

blocks.title: Trap
blocks.text: The PM does not approve the charter. The sponsor authorises it; the PM is named in it.

sections.heading: Governance & decision rights
sections.body: Governance is the framework of authority around the project: who makes which decisions, who is accountable, how issues escalate, and how the project connects to the sponsor, steering committee or board, and the wider organisation. Good governance gives clear accountability and a defined path for resolving issues and approving changes, so decisions are made at the right level rather than stalling or being made by the wrong people. Governance is the rules of the road; management is the day-to-day driving. The PM operates within the decision rights that governance defines.

blocks.title: Remember
lines: Governance sets WHO decides and HOW issues escalate - not the work itself.
The PM works within, not above, the decision rights governance defines.

sections.heading: Phase-gate (stage-gate) reviews
sections.body: Predictive and hybrid projects are often split into phases separated by gates. A gate is a formal checkpoint, not just a milestone. At each gate, leadership reviews the completed deliverables and re-tests whether the business case is still valid, then makes a decision before more money is committed. Gates control investment incrementally and keep the project aligned to expected value - if the business case has collapsed, the gate is where you stop spending.

figure.caption: Phases (boxes) are separated by gates (diamonds) where the project is continued or stopped.

items.title: Go
lines: Deliverables sound and business case valid - continue and commit the next tranche.

items.title: Hold
lines: Pause and revisit before committing more.

items.title: Recycle
lines: Send back for rework, then re-review.

items.title: Kill
lines: Business case no longer holds - stop spending.

blocks.title: A gate asks two questions
lines: Are the deliverables sound?
Is the business case still worth funding?

sections.heading: Artifacts by type -> purpose
sections.body: PMBOK 8 groups artifacts by type rather than by knowledge area. Recognise which family an artifact belongs to and what it is for. PMI keeps RAID items as separate artifacts even though the real world often consolidates them into a single RAID log.

blocks.title: Logs and registers -> track ongoing items

rows.value: Future, uncertain events; probability, impact, response, owner.
Problems happening now that need action.
Assumptions and constraints being relied on.
Who stakeholders are and their interest/influence.
Requested changes and their disposition.

blocks.title: Plans -> define how work is done

rows.value: How each aspect (scope, schedule, cost, quality, etc.) is managed.
How to engage stakeholders (vs the register, which lists who they are).
Approved scope statement, WBS and WBS dictionary.
Approved schedule to measure against.
Approved time-phased budget to measure against.
Feed Monitoring & Controlling with reliable, actionable status.
Authorises the project (see above).
Justifies the investment; re-tested at each gate.
Decomposes scope - covered in the Schedule & Network Diagrams guide.

blocks.title: Dependencies
blocks.text: Dependencies are tracked within the schedule, not a standalone PMI artifact - the D in a RAID log.

sections.heading: RAID: risk vs issue
sections.body: A RAID log is a single consolidated view of Risks, Assumptions, Issues and Dependencies, used in the real world to keep all four visible in one place. For the exam, know the nuance: PMI formally keeps these as separate artifacts (risk register, assumption log, issue log, and dependencies in the schedule). Treat RAID as a practical consolidation, but recognise the individual PMI documents. The two most often confused are risk and issue.

groups.label: Definition
lines: A future, uncertain event that may or may not happen.

groups.label: Lives in
lines: The risk register.

groups.label: Definition
lines: Something already happening now that needs action.

groups.label: Lives in
lines: The issue log.

blocks.title: Don't merge them
blocks.text: Risk = future uncertainty (risk register). Issue = happening now (issue log).

sections.heading: Baseline vs register updates
sections.body: The single most important governance distinction for artifacts: registers and logs are updated freely as new information arrives, but a baseline is the approved yardstick and can only change through the formal change-control process.

rows.value: Updated freely and continuously - add risks, issues, assumptions, stakeholders as they emerge. No change request needed.
Approved reference for scope, schedule or cost. Changing it requires an approved change request.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Never edit a baseline directly.
Baseline change -> raise a change request; governance/CCB approves before the baseline moves.
Updating a register does NOT need a change request.

sections.heading: The change-control path
sections.body: When a change touches an approved baseline, follow the governed process rather than acting directly. This keeps authority, value and accountability connected.

items.title: Capture the request
lines: Log it in the change log; describe the change and its trigger.

items.title: Assess the impact
lines: Evaluate effect on scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk and value.

items.title: Route for decision
lines: Take it to the approver defined by governance (e.g. CCB or sponsor).

items.title: Update baselines if approved
lines: Only after approval, re-baseline and communicate the new reference.

items.title: Trace it back
lines: Link the change to the business case and charter so value stays connected.

blocks.title: Governance principle
blocks.text: Decisions are made at the right level; escalate only when the normal path cannot resolve it.

sections.heading: How the artifacts work together
sections.body: These documents form a chain of authority and control. The business case justifies the work and the charter authorises it; governance defines who may decide what; gates re-confirm value at set points; and the RAID artifacts keep risks, assumptions, issues and dependencies under continuous control. Scope is decomposed separately in the Work Breakdown Structure - see the Schedule & Network Diagrams guide for the WBS in full. Every change request and decision should be traceable back through this structure, so authority, value and accountability stay connected from start to close.

items.title: Business case
lines: Justifies the investment.

items.title: Charter
lines: Authorises the project and the PM.

items.title: Governance
lines: Decides who approves what.

items.title: Gates
lines: Confirm value before committing more.

items.title: RAID + baselines
lines: Keep risks and the approved yardstick under control.

blocks.title: One-line recall
blocks.text: Business case justifies, charter authorises, governance decides, gates confirm value - and baselines only move by change request.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam and aligned to PMBOK 8 domains and processes.
